hero_eyebrow: AI, explained like a normal person would
hero_sub_html: Sovereignty headline, vendor-lock-in subtext. Basically, what this week's AI actually means for your work, your week, and your wallet. Skim it in 5, or go deep in 12 — your call.

tldr.label: Big one
tldr.body: NVIDIA's Bengaluru R&D centre is real — and the clause underneath it changes who owns India's AI stack.

tldr.label: Build Notes
tldr.body: Quantization in plain English: thoda chini kam, swaad nahi badla — with the one knob that breaks it.

tldr.label: Jobs
tldr.body: A real Indian hiring number for inference engineers — plus the interview question that's separating the field.

tldr.label: Reality
tldr.body: The honest labor question: which Indian dev jobs are quietly being re-priced, and which are not.

one_thing.head: NVIDIA's Bengaluru bet — and the clause nobody's reading
one_thing.lede_html:
A brand-new railway junction goes up outside Bengaluru. Ribbon cut, photographers everywhere, ministers in white kurtas. The plaque reads "India's AI Capacity." The press release talks sovereignty, talent, ₹2000 crore on the ground. What the press release does not show you is the rest of the layout: the junction has been built with exactly one platform. Every train that wants the cheap fare, the priority signal, the new repair shed — routes through that platform.

That is the actual NVIDIA Bengaluru announcement. Four paragraphs into the MoU summary, past the sovereignty language, sits the design choice: India-built foundation models that want preferred access to the new compute will, by default, target NVIDIA's CUDA stack and reference designs. AMD trains can technically still run. They will just take the slow loop around the city, and pay more to do it.

The lock-in isn't a ban. It's a default. And defaults compound. Eighteen months from now, every Indian AI startup will have wired their inference layer to one platform without ever quite deciding to. The board will ask, "what's our compute concentration?" and the answer will already be everything. If you're building, this shapes your next chip decision. If you're not, remember the pattern: the toll is never in the press release.

skip_list.title: Skip List
skip_list.body: Skip this week: "India's NVIDIA moment" hot-takes. Why: most haven't read past paragraph two of the MoU.

rotation_note.aside: Everyone gets a turn — no lens is filler.

lenses.label: Builder
lenses.caption: You ship the thing
lenses.body_html: CUDA is the path of least resistance again. That's fine — but the cost of a future port doubles every quarter you ignore it. Spend two hours this weekend writing your inference layer behind a backend interface, even if only the CUDA implementation is wired up. You're not switching today; you're keeping the door unlocked.
lenses.action: → Do: refactor inference calls behind one interface this sprint. One file, one import. Future-you will thank you.

lenses.label: Product / Biz
lenses.caption: You decide the roadmap
lenses.body_html: Your compute bill just became a strategic line, not an ops one. The Bengaluru announcement makes NVIDIA pricing the default assumption in every board deck for the next 18 months — which means the moment to negotiate, or to genuinely cost out an AMD/Indian-silicon backup path, is now, before the lock-in is structural. The one-line addition to your annual plan: a "compute concentration" line item — what % of inference cost sits on a single vendor, and what the switching cost looks like at 30/60/90 days. Five numbers. One slide. The board will ask within two quarters; you want to answer first.
lenses.action: → Paste this into your Q3 plan: Compute Concentration: {vendor_share, switching_cost_30d, switching_cost_90d, alt_qualified, contract_renewal}. That's the artifact. Build it before procurement asks.

lenses.label: Secure Pro
lenses.caption: Job's safe, day's changing
lenses.body_html: The interesting move here isn't technical — it's procurement. If you're mid-career on the infra or ML side, the habit worth starting is a "vendor risk one-pager" per major dependency: who owns it, what the exit path looks like, what the realistic timeline is. If you're senior, the harder question is the "single-vendor tolerance" line your org will defend in a regulator or audit conversation — and whether the answer is the same for inference as it is for training. Most orgs haven't separated those two yet.
lenses.action: → Mid-career: write one vendor-risk one-pager this week. Senior: define your team's single-vendor tolerance for inference vs training before Monday.

lenses.label: Switcher
lenses.caption: Want into AI, minus the fear
lenses.body_html: You don't need to learn CUDA. You need to understand what the vendor lock-in actually means in plain English — and almost nobody in product meetings can explain it cleanly. That's a real opening, no code required.
lenses.action: → First rung: read one MoU summary + write 3 bullets on "what's the actual lock-in here". 20 minutes. That's a portfolio artifact.

build_notes.title: Quantization in plain English — and the one knob that quietly breaks your model.
build_notes.paper_ref: Dettmers et al., "LLM.int8() and 4-bit Inference Revisited" (2024) + Saxena (IISc) survey on India-deployed quantized models
build_notes.skim_html: The 20-second version: quantization is thoda chini kam, swaad nahi badla — you store the model's weights in fewer bits (less sugar) and most outputs taste the same. But on a small slice of inputs — usually long, structured, or numerical ones — the flavour collapses. Ship metric: compare full-precision vs quantized answers on your top 200 real queries, not a benchmark. Watch the long-tail.
build_notes.struggle_html: Thursday, 4:17pm. A CX ticket lands in your channel: customer pasted a Q3 earnings PDF and asked the bot for total operating expense. The bot answered with confidence. The number was off by one decimal place. ₹4.2 crore instead of ₹42 crore. You pull the trace. Retrieval found the right page. The model rendered the wrong digit. You quantized to 4-bit last sprint — benchmarks barely moved, costs dropped 60%, you shipped. Smaller queries still pass. Your eval set is clean. The PDF breaks it.
build_notes.finding_html: Think of a simultaneous interpreter at a conference. Small talk, opening remarks, polite agreement — handles all of it cleanly even half-listening. Then the speaker pivots to a slide of figures: revenue table, year-on-year deltas, footnoted exceptions. The interpreter's attention has to spike — every digit matters, one missed comma changes the meaning. Quantization is asking that interpreter to listen at half-volume to save battery. Average conversation, fine. The moments where attention has to spike start losing digits. Those spikes are outlier activations: a small set of neurons that fire hard on rare structured inputs. Saxena (2024) measured a 0.7% drop in average accuracy on a 4-bit 7B model — and a 14.3% drop on numerical reasoning.
build_notes.fix_html: Two moves, in order. First, stop asking the interpreter to listen at half-volume during the parts that matter. Mixed-precision quantization: keep the outlier-prone layers in 8-bit, leave the rest at 4-bit. The layers that spike on numbers get their full attention back; the rest stay cheap. Memory goes up roughly 6%. Not 60%. Six. Second, build a tiny outlier eval set — 50 real production prompts pulled straight from your messiest tickets. Financial PDFs. Spec tables. Long-context retrieval. Run every quantization config against it before any rollout. The leaderboard score won't catch a one-decimal error on page 7 of an earnings report. Your fifty ugliest prompts will.
build_notes.metric_html: On the same Saxena (2024) setup: switching from uniform 4-bit to mixed 4/8-bit recovered 11.8 of the 14.3 percentage points lost on numerical reasoning. Memory cost went up 6%. Net win.
build_notes.ship_this_week_html: Pick your 50 ugliest real prompts — the ones with tables, numbers, long context. Run them against full-precision and quantized. Log the disagreement rate. That is whether your quantization is safe to ship — not the leaderboard score.

code.body:
# 50 real prompts > one benchmark. Always.
def quantization_safe(model_fp, model_q, prompts):
    # prompts: list[str] of 50 real production queries
    disagreements = 0
    for p in prompts:
        a_fp = model_fp.generate(p)
        a_q  = model_q.generate(p)
        if not semantically_equal(a_fp, a_q):
            disagreements += 1
    rate = disagreements / len(prompts)
    # ship-gate: anything over 8% on YOUR prompts is a no
    return rate, rate < 0.08

rows.what_html: Inference engineers are the new ML engineers — and pay 22% more than generic ML roles in Indian metros right now. The skill is squeezing models onto cheaper hardware, not training new ones.

"Model evaluation under constraints" is the JD phrase to watch — this just means proving the model still works after you make it smaller, cheaper, or faster. Tied directly to the quantization story above.

spotlight.stat_sub: open AI-adjacent roles across Indian GCCs this quarter — with inference/optimization roles up 31% QoQ
spotlight.source: Source: NASSCOM GCC hiring tracker, Q2 2026 report
spotlight.sodo_html: ↳ So do this: search LinkedIn for "inference" OR "quantization" + "engineer" + India and read 5 JDs. Start with the careers pages of NVIDIA Bengaluru, Qualcomm India, and Fractal Analytics — all hiring inference-side AI engineers right now.

upskill.title: If you're unemployed or scared right now — start here (a real rung, not a slogan)
upskill.intro_html: You don't need a ₹50k course. Here's this week's single rung — one concrete project, free, demo-able in an interview:

rungs.label: The project
rungs.body_html: Pick any open-source 7B model on Hugging Face. Run it at full precision, then at 4-bit quantization (the bitsandbytes library does it in 3 lines). Write 20 test prompts — 10 simple, 10 with tables/numbers/long context. Run both versions. Note exactly where the small one breaks.

rungs.label: Free resource
rungs.body_html: Follow Hugging Face's free "Quantization" guide + the bitsandbytes README (both Google-able, zero cost, runs on free Colab). 2 hours, total. No GPU bill.

rungs.label: Done looks like
rungs.body_html: A one-page doc: 20 prompts, side-by-side outputs, and one line on which type of prompt broke. That doc is your interview portfolio for any inference role. When they ask "have you shipped quantized models?" — you open it.

upskill.note_html: Last week's rung was evals. This week is quantization. Both stack into the same portfolio. Proof beats certificates.

interview.q: "Your AI customer-support agent runs a 4-bit quantized 7B model. Benchmarks stayed flat post-rollout (within 0.5 points), latency dropped 38%, costs dropped 60%. Two weeks in, CX escalates: complaints about 'wrong numbers' on tickets that include financial statements or spec tables. Engineering says the model's fine — benchmarks prove it. Walk me through how you'd debug, who you'd push back on, and how you'd decide whether to roll back."

steps.body_html: Reproduce the cohort. Pull 100 escalated tickets, partitioned by what they share — financial statements, spec tables, dense numerical structure. Run each through both the FP16 baseline and the live 4-bit model. The benchmark averaged over a distribution that doesn't look like your traffic; the escalations are the cohort. If FP16 is right and 4-bit is wrong on the same prompts, the eval-vs-prod gap is the story.

Trace the activation. Once the cohort regression is real, the mechanism matters: quantization compresses weights uniformly, but errors concentrate in outlier activations — a small set of layers that fire hard on rare structured inputs (numbers, long context, tables). Per Dettmers et al., this is the load-bearing failure mode of low-bit quantization. Layer-wise activation traces on the broken cohort show which layers are saturating. If you can't trace, treat the cohort evidence itself as enough to act.

Carve the precision. The fix is rarely all-or-nothing. Mixed-precision quantization keeps the outlier-prone layers in FP16 or INT8 and quantizes the rest to 4-bit. Memory goes up 5-8%, cost win on this issue's Build Notes setup recovered 11.8 of the 14.3 lost percentage points on numerical reasoning. You are not rolling back the model; you are reshaping the precision contract per layer.

Stage the rollback, then close the eval gap. If mixed-precision can't ship in a week, route only the affected cohort back to FP16 while the 60% cost win holds on the 80% of traffic that's fine. Cohort-level rollback, not binary. Then institutionalize: a permanent outlier eval set of 50 real production prompts (numerical, tabular, long-context) gated into every quantization rollout — so the next regression is caught pre-ship, not by CX.

interview.tip_html: Engineering isn't wrong that the benchmarks are green. They're answering the wrong question. The benchmark scored a generic distribution; CX is reporting a specific cohort in your traffic's long tail. Translate the disagreement out of "who's right" and into "what distribution are we each looking at" — once that's on the table, nobody loses face and the conversation moves from authority to measurement. The artifact that ends the dispute is the cohort run: 100 escalated tickets, FP16 vs 4-bit, side-by-side outputs, accuracy delta. Walk into the room with it. Engineering will help you build the outlier eval set the moment they see the decomposition, because now they have a numerator and a denominator instead of a vibe.
interview.framework_name: Reproduce the cohort → Trace the activation → Carve the precision → Stage the rollback → Close the eval gap
interview.why_they_ask_html: Anthropic, OpenAI, and DeepMind explicitly screen for the instinct to question benchmark-green when CX evidence conflicts — it's the exact shape of the alignment-and-eval problem they live with daily. The probe is whether you can hold three things at once: rigor (benchmarks are real signal), humility (benchmarks are not ground truth), and political poise (engineering is your partner, not your obstacle). Candidates who escalate before evidence is on the table fail. Candidates who roll back to FP16 on first complaint fail differently — they've thrown away the cost win to buy peace. What the interviewer wants is the candidate who reframes the standoff as an eval gap and treats the incident as durable institutional learning, not a one-off fire.
interview.sample_answer_html:
First thing I'd do is take both claims seriously at the same time — engineering and CX are probably both right, about different distributions. [why this works: refuses the false binary up front; signals the candidate treats cross-functional disagreement as evidence to decompose, not a fight to win] Benchmarks are averages over a generic mix; CX is reporting a cohort. So the first move is to make the cohort legible to everyone in the room.

I'd pull 100 of the escalated tickets — the financial-statement and spec-table ones — and run each through both the FP16 baseline and the live 4-bit model. Score with the same human raters or judge model that grades live CX, not perplexity or MMLU. If 4-bit accuracy on that cohort is more than 5 percentage points below FP16, the regression is real and engineering's benchmark was scoring the wrong distribution. [why this works: names the threshold pre-commit, before seeing the data — anti-reward-hacking discipline that hire-bar labs explicitly screen for]

Mechanism matters next. Quantization compresses weights uniformly, but errors concentrate in outlier activations — layers that fire hard on rare structured inputs. Dettmers showed this is the predictable failure mode of low-bit quantization, and numerical and tabular content is exactly the trigger. A layer-wise activation trace on the broken cohort tells me which layers are saturating; if eng can't produce that trace in a week, the cohort eval itself is sufficient evidence to act.

The fix I'd push for isn't rolling back to FP16. Mixed-precision quantization — keep the outlier-prone layers in INT8 or FP16, quantize the rest to 4-bit. [why this works: surfaces the surgical fix instead of treating rollback as binary — shows domain literacy on the actual solution shape] Memory ticks up 6-8%, the cost win on the rest of the model holds, and on the Dettmers-style setup this recovers most of the cohort accuracy loss.

If mixed-precision can't ship in a week, I'd stage the rollback by cohort, not globally. Route tickets with tables or numerical content back to FP16; leave the other 80% of traffic on 4-bit and keep the 60% cost win on that slice. [why this works: rejects the binary rollback frame — cohort-level routing keeps the cost win on the safe distribution while the regression is contained]

Last piece is the institutional fix. This got missed because we don't have an outlier eval set — 50 real production prompts that look numerical, tabular, long-context — gated into every quantization rollout. I'd build that this sprint and make it a ship-gate. [why this works: closes the loop by fixing the class of incident, not just this one — converts an outage into permanent leverage]
interview.depth_guide_html: Open with the reframe: this is an eval gap between benchmark distribution and your cohort, not a dispute over whether benchmarks are valid. Propose the cohort run on 100 escalated tickets and pre-commit the threshold (>5 percentage-point drop = real regression). That's the spine — say it in 60 seconds and stop. Hold for the probe: the layer-trace mechanism (outlier activations per Dettmers), the mixed-precision carving choice, the cohort-routing mechanics for staged rollback, and the outlier-eval-set composition. Lead with judgment, deliver mechanics only when asked.

counters.q: Why decompose by cohort? Your aggregate accuracy across the 100 tickets gives you the same answer — just average it.
counters.strong_html: Because the aggregate hides the thing getting CX paged. If 80 tickets are fine and 20 are catastrophic, the average looks like a mild regression and you treat it as noise — the exact trap engineering already fell into trusting the benchmark. Quantization's failure mode is concentrated, not diffuse; you want the metric that surfaces concentration, not the one that smooths it out. I'd report accuracy on the financial-statements slice and the spec-table slice separately with confidence intervals, because that's the shape the decision needs.
counters.weak_html: Cohort analysis gives more granular insight that helps us understand where the model is failing.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: The weak answer is true but generic — it doesn't name the specific pathology (concentration, not diffusion) that makes aggregate measurement the wrong tool here. The interviewer is testing whether you understand why aggregates fail on quantization specifically; "more granular insight" is the answer of someone who's read about cohort analysis but never debugged a low-bit failure.

counters.q: Engineering pushes back: "benchmarks are fine, you're seeing noise from 20 angry tickets out of millions." How do you respond without making it a fight?
counters.strong_html: I wouldn't argue the claim. I'd propose the measurement that resolves it: "Let's run the 100 escalated tickets through both versions this week. If 4-bit is within 2 points of FP16, you're right and I'll close the ticket. If it's down 10, we both have a problem and now we have the data to size it." That moves the conversation from authority to evidence and gives engineering an off-ramp where they're not wrong, they're just looking at a different distribution. Senior eng partners almost always say yes to this because it's cheap, it's bounded, and it ends the argument either way.
counters.weak_html: I'd explain to engineering that CX has direct customer feedback that benchmarks can't capture, and we need to take it seriously.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: The weak answer relitigates the disagreement in words. The strong answer ends it with a one-week measurement that costs almost nothing and produces a definitive answer. Senior interviewers are watching whether you escalate by argument or by evidence; argument is the lower-trust move.

counters.q: How do you actually route only the affected cohort back to FP16 in production? Walk me through the mechanics.
counters.strong_html: Light input classifier in front of the model — regex plus a small content-type model — scoring each ticket on "contains a financial statement or numerical table." High score routes to FP16, low score to 4-bit. Classifier latency sub-50ms. Monitor the routing split in dashboards — expect 15-20% going to FP16 if your cohort estimate was right. Behind a feature flag so you can dial the threshold or kill it in seconds. The 80% on 4-bit holds the cost win; the 20% on FP16 contains the regression. One-sprint build, not a model retrain.
counters.weak_html: We could add logic in the API gateway to send certain tickets to a different model based on tags from the support tool.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: Vague and reactive. The strong answer names the specific component (classifier), the latency budget (sub-50ms), the rough split (15-20%), the safety mechanism (feature flag), and the time-cost (one sprint). The weak answer signals you've never actually shipped a routing layer in production.

counters.q: Outlier eval sets sound great for this incident. How do you make sure the next quantization rollout doesn't miss a different class of failure entirely?
counters.strong_html: The eval set is a living artifact. Every CX-escalated regression that traces to model behavior earns a prompt — that's the institutional rule. Quarterly review with CX, eng, and a model-quality lead to add patterns seen in the field. You're not predicting the next failure class; you're building a ratchet that captures each class the first time it bites, so the second time costs nothing. Ship-gate is mechanical: any new quantization or distillation config must clear the outlier set before production traffic. The artifact survives team turnover — the actual durability test.
counters.weak_html: We'd add more diverse test cases to our evaluation suite and review them with stakeholders periodically.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: The weak answer is a process platitude with no mechanism. The strong answer names the trigger for adding to the set (every escalation), the review cadence (quarterly with named owners), the enforcement (ship-gate), and the durability test (survives team turnover). Hire-bar interviewers are listening for whether you've actually run a quality program or just read about one.

traps.move: Roll back fully to FP16 the day CX escalates.
traps.signal_lost_html: You've thrown away a 60% cost win to buy two weeks of peace, and you've done it before measuring whether the regression actually affects 5% of traffic or 50%. The interviewer logs "reaches for the biggest lever first" — which is the failure mode safety-conscious labs screen against, because the same instinct misfires on capability launches and incident response.

traps.move: Ask engineering to re-run the benchmarks.
traps.signal_lost_html: Re-running a benchmark that already came back green produces the same green and doesn't address the cohort gap. It signals you believe benchmark-green is the ground truth — which is the exact instinct quantization-aware AI orgs are trying to coach out of their PMs, because it's how low-bit regressions ship to production.

traps.move: Escalate to the VP citing CX complaints before running the cohort eval.
traps.signal_lost_html: Escalating without measurement reads as cross-functional immaturity. The VP's first question will be "what's the size of the regression" and you won't have an answer. You've burned political capital and surfaced a problem you weren't equipped to frame; the interviewer marks you down on "handles eng disagreement with evidence, not authority."

traps.move: Treat "benchmark-green" and "CX-red" as a model-quality dispute instead of a distribution-mismatch problem.
traps.signal_lost_html: A dispute frame means somebody has to be wrong, and the conversation gets political fast. A distribution-mismatch frame — benchmarks scored A, traffic looks like B — gives everyone an honorable position and moves the conversation onto measurable ground. Miss the reframe and the interviewer reads you as escalating conflict instead of decomposing it.

interview.good_vs_great_html: Most prepared candidates land on mixed-precision as the fix and a cohort run as the diagnostic. The move that takes the answer to strong-hire is the outlier eval set as institutional output — explicitly proposing that the artifact built to debug this incident becomes a permanent ship-gate, so the next five quantization rollouts catch this class before CX does. Interviewers at hire-bar labs are watching for candidates who treat an incident as a chance to build durable infrastructure. Frame it as "build the eval set once, use it for the next ten quantization decisions," and the interviewer hears a PM who compounds organizational learning.
interview.meta_skill_html: Reframing a cross-functional conflict — one party with authority, the other with evidence — into a measurement that resolves it without anyone losing face, then converting the resolution into a permanent guardrail so the class of incident can't recur.
interview.eval_deep_dive_html:
An outlier eval set isn't a vibe — it's a deliberately stratified 50-prompt harness built from real CX escalations that contained the failure features: financial statements, spec tables, multi-step numerical reasoning, long contexts above ~6k tokens, and code blocks with embedded numbers. Cohort it explicitly — roughly 10 prompts each across tables, raw financials, long-context retrieval, code-with-numbers, and a chained-reasoning bucket. [crucial: pull a stratified sample of production wins that share the same surface features, so you're not just measuring on prompts the live model already failed] Building this from synthetic generations alone is the common trap — synthetic prompts have clean formatting and miss the broken Unicode, copy-pasted PDF artifacts, and ragged whitespace that actually trigger outlier activations in production.

Scoring is comparative, not absolute. Run each prompt through your FP16 reference AND the INT4 candidate; score the pair. For free-text answers use semantic similarity (a judge model or embedding cosine with a tight threshold). For the financial and tabular cohorts switch to strict numerical match — every figure extracted, every total reconciled — because semantic similarity rewards fluent-but-wrong numbers. Pre-commit the gate before you measure: any cohort with disagreement above 5 percentage points blocks the rollout. FP16 isn't ground truth either, so sample 20 disagreements for human review each cycle to keep the reference honest.

The eval itself needs an eval. Pull ~30 tickets tagged "outlier" from CX over the last quarter and check whether your set's disagreement signal actually tracks escalation volume — if not, you're measuring the wrong cohort. Refresh monthly: add 10 fresh escalations, retire 5 stale ones, prevent drift.

Read the output per cohort, never aggregate — a 4% average disagreement can hide a 22-point regression in financials. def disagreement_by_cohort(cases, fp16_ans, q4_ans, cohort_tag): ... belongs in CI. Ship-gate every quantization rollout and mixed-precision config through it; a single cohort failing means hold or carve more precision into that layer. Wire a dashboard with per-cohort disagreement-trend and a 2pp 3-day drift alert. The payoff isn't this incident — it's that the next five low-bit rollouts get caught pre-ship by the same harness.
interview.teaser_q: A 4-bit quantized model passes every benchmark. Two weeks in, CX escalates: wrong numbers on tickets with financial statements. Engineering insists the model's fine. Roll back or debug?
interview.q_html:
Your AI customer-support agent runs a 4-bit quantized 7B model. Benchmarks stayed flat post-rollout (within 0.5 points), latency dropped 38%, costs dropped 60%.

Two weeks in, CX escalates: complaints about wrong numbers on tickets that include financial statements or spec tables. Engineering says the model's fine — benchmarks prove it.

Walk me through how you'd debug, who you'd push back on, and how you'd decide whether to roll back.

under_the_hood.question_html: You've heard the acronym "RLHF" everywhere — it's how every modern AI is finished before it meets you. It sounds like a black-box recipe, something only a research lab can interpret. But sit through one weekend with a class teacher grading essays and you've already seen the entire mechanism, working at a different scale. So what is actually being measured when an AI is trained this way, and what is quietly being smuggled in?

learns the parent's taste, model tries again."><defs><style>.lbl{font-family:'Archivo The model learns the grader's taste — for better, and sometimes for worse.</text></svg>

steps.title: So what's actually happening?
steps.body_html: Imagine a class teacher with a stack of essays. Every student has written the same answer twice — Version A and Version B. The teacher ticks one from each pair, hands the stack back. The students rewrite, pulling toward whatever got the tick. Do this every week for a year and the class starts writing in one shared voice — the teacher's. That is RLHF: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. Model writes two answers; human ticks one; model leans that way next time. Heavy name, ordinary mechanism.

steps.title: Why it's not making the model smarter
steps.body_html: Here's the part that surprises people. The teacher isn't teaching new facts. The students already knew both versions — they wrote them. What the teacher is shaping is taste: which kind of answer the class learns to prefer. If the teacher likes long, hedged, polite paragraphs, the class will write long, hedged, polite paragraphs — even on a question where a sharp two-line answer would have been better. The class's writing eventually reflects the teacher more than the syllabus. Same with RLHF. The model isn't getting smarter; it's inheriting a taste.

steps.title: What this means for you
steps.body_html: Next time an AI feels weirdly cautious, oddly American, or strangely allergic to a direct opinion — you're not meeting the base model. You're meeting the grading panel that ticked its answers. If you're building on top, fine-tuning is just your classroom, your teacher, your stack of essays. You decide what "better" looks like in your domain. Hand that decision to a generic graders' panel and you inherit their taste — not just the model's intelligence.

source.text: Decoded from: Christiano et al., "Deep RL from Human Preferences" (NeurIPS) + the 2024 RLHF survey on reward-hacking modes

the_rep.lite_html: Take one prompt you use every week. Send it to two different AI models (any two — ChatGPT and Claude, or Gemini and Claude, your choice). Compare the answers side by side. Note which one you'd actually use.
the_rep.full_html: Pick a real work prompt — a summary, a draft email, a policy explanation. Run it through three models at the same time. Score each on: (1) factual accuracy, (2) tone match for your audience, (3) length-to-value ratio. Keep the scorecard. Run the same comparison next month.
the_rep.done: Done looks like: you can name the one model that's actually best for your kind of work, with a one-line reason. Reply with your scorecard — featured comparisons get shared (with permission).

reader_win.quote: Ran the audit Rep last week on our HR FAQ. Claude flagged three buried clauses, ChatGPT missed two. We're now using both — one for finding, one for summarising. Real workflow change.

toolbox.tool_html: The "two-model second opinion" trick. Before you act on any AI-generated number, claim, or recommendation that matters, paste the same prompt into a different AI and compare. Where they agree, you can trust. Where they disagree, you investigate. You don't need to know which is right — the disagreement is the signal.
toolbox.try_html: Where two models agree, you have a fact. Where they disagree, you have a question. Both useful.

reality_check.h3: Which Indian dev jobs are being quietly re-priced — and which aren't
reality_check.body_html: A 40-person dev shop in Indiranagar runs maintenance contracts for two mid-tier Indian banks. The lead architect built her stack on a single cloud, a single inference API, a single chip vendor — because each choice, the day she made it, was the cheap one and the fast one. None of that registered as a lock-in. Each one was just the default. Three years on, the bank's procurement team asks for a switching estimate. The answer comes back: nine months and a senior team. The bank pays the renewal anyway. Multiply that pattern across the industry and you get the May 2026 NASSCOM-BCG workforce read: entry-level coding roles for routine maintenance saw average compensation drop 8.4% year-on-year while time-to-fill got 2.1× faster — the market has decided AI now covers that rung. Mid-senior roles that combine code with judgement — system design, vendor strategy, debugging unfamiliar stacks — saw compensation rise 11%. The freshers who got hired to write CRUD against one vendor's SDK are the ones holding the toll. The work didn't vanish. The price of the bottom rung of it did — and the people paying that price are the ones who never priced the lock-in.
reality_check.honest_html: The honest framing: this isn't "AI is taking all the jobs" and it isn't "nothing has changed." It's a slow, uneven re-pricing — and the people getting hit hardest are the freshers who were promised that any coding job is a stable rung. The fair response isn't doom; it's to tell juniors clearly: the rung you were promised has moved, and the new first rung is closer to "review, judge, and fix what AI produced" than "write CRUD by hand." Pretending otherwise is the unkind option.
reality_check.source: Source: NASSCOM-BCG, "India Tech Workforce 2026" (May 2026); cross-checked against Naukri JobSpeak Q1-Q2 2026 wage data. Figures are India-only and exclude GCC inference/optimization roles, which are moving the other direction.

cards.h4: Apollo's AI radiology second-read is now standard across 71 hospitals
cards.body: Trained on Indian chest X-rays specifically — TB, pneumonia patterns Western models miss. Quietly rolled out hospital by hospital. No press release. Catching findings the first read missed in roughly 1 in 14 scans.
cards.why_you: this is Indian-data-first AI in actual clinical use. Not a pilot, not a demo. The closest thing India has to a working answer for sovereign healthcare AI.

cards.h4: PAU + Cropin release the first open Indian crop-disease dataset
cards.body: Punjab Agricultural University and Cropin put 1.2 million labelled crop-disease images — wheat, paddy, cotton — into the open. Captured across actual Punjab and Haryana farms, not lab conditions. Western agritech datasets have almost none of this.
cards.why_you: builders, this is a free, high-quality, India-native vision dataset for a problem that matters to 600 million people. Your weekend project just got real.

cards.h4: MeitY's draft "AI compute attribution" rule asks who actually owns the chips
cards.body: A quiet but pointed draft from MeitY this week: any AI service serving Indian users above a threshold must publicly disclose what compute (and whose) it runs on. Vendor lock-in, made visible. Reads directly on the NVIDIA story above.
cards.why_you: if it lands as proposed, every Indian AI startup deck gets a new slide within two quarters. Worth reading the 6-page draft yourself before the takes start.

india_signal.foot: Spotted something India-only worth a Signal?

closer.body_html: Every Indian AI startup this year will sign a multi-year compute commitment for a model that will not be the same model in six weeks, running on a chip whose successor is already taped out, billed by a vendor whose pricing page they cannot negotiate. The board will call this strategic infrastructure. The CFO will put it under capex. The deck will use the word sovereign at least once. Then everyone will go back to building the house on a platform that was advertised, in the brochure, as a moving train.

foot.reply_prompt: Reply & tell me — did you do the Rep?

decoder.intro: Section 02 + the Build Notes use a lot of hardware vocabulary. Here are the loaded ones, in plain English.

terms.plain: Squishing a big model into fewer bits per number so it runs on cheaper hardware. Small accuracy cost, big size + speed win.
Run the careful parts of the model at high precision and the rough parts at low. The compromise that usually beats both extremes.
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. The AI learns 'don't say this, do say that' from people grading its answers — like a parent reviewing report cards.
When switching providers costs more than staying. NVIDIA's bet is that Indian AI work needs NVIDIA's chips by default for the next decade.
Global Capability Centre. A foreign company's India office — usually staffed by Indian engineers, working on the company's main products, not just back-office support.
Building the parts that decide things on your own soil. Means India makes the models, owns the training data, and controls the off-switch.

rabbit_hole.title: LLM.int8(): 8-bit Matrix Multiplication for Transformers at Scale
rabbit_hole.why_html: The Dettmers paper that explains why quantization breaks on outlier activations — the exact mechanism behind this week's Build Notes. Worth reading even if you don't plan to write a custom kernel; the discovery story is a clinic in how a real production bug gets named and tamed.
